coredata.dc:description: The total antioxidant level is an important biomarker to determine redox imbalance in human body which can cause many diseases and threaten human health. Though the detection of antioxidants in vivo is gaining wide attention, most of the developed methods have only one detection mode and can only detect single type of antioxidant, leading to inaccurate test results. Here, we developed CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs as a dual-modal probe for water soluble total antioxidants detection in saliva. The detection mechanism was based on two reactions, including a cascading reaction of antioxidants activating the production of free radicals for fluorescence components generated, and a coordination reaction of antioxidants capturing Cu2+ from the MOFs nodes for carboxyl group protonated. Based on this, the UV–vis absorbance intensity and fluorescence intensity of the probes would be simultaneously changed after the incubation with antioxidants. Thus, the probe can detect antioxidants through both UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers. The dual-modal detection method can achieve rapid detection within 10 min with only 200 μL of samples used. Water soluble total antioxidants in saliva with component proportion fluctuated within a certain range can be well detected. And the obtained results were accurate with high recovery. This dual-modal probe provided an accurate, rapid and noninvasive method to detect water soluble total antioxidants in human body.

TEM images of CuBDC MOFs (e), (f) and CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs (g), (h). (i) EDS and (j) corresponding EDS-elemental mappings of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. (k) PXRD patterns of CuBDC MOFs and CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. (l) FTIR spectra of CuBDC MOFs and CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. (m) XPS spectra of CuBDC MOFs and CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. (n) High-resolution Fe2p of CuBDC MOFs and CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs.

(a) The mechanism of antioxidants detection based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of K3[Fe(CN)6] mixed with supernate extracted from the solution containing CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs and different antioxidants (* represent CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART). The embedded photograph displayed the color of the mixed solution containing K3[Fe(CN)6] and the supernate (K: K3[Fe(CN)6], U: UA, A: ASC, L: L-Cys, G: GSH). (e) Powder X-ray diffraction pattern for CuBDC MOFs, CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs, CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs & GSH, CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs & L-Cys, CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs & ASC and CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs & UA. High-resolution Cu2p of the reaction product between CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs and GSH (f), the reaction product between CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs and L-Cys (g), the reaction product between CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs and UA (h).

The UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and fluorescence spectra (c) of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs solution with GSH added in aqueous solution. The linear relationship between absorbance intensity change and GSH concentration (b) or between fluorescence intensity change and GSH concentration (d) (ranges from 0-0.1 mM) in aqueous solutions.

The linear relationship between UV–vis absorbance intensity change and GSH (a), L-Cys (c), ASC (e) and UA (g) concentration or between fluorescence intensity change and GSH (b), L-Cys (d), ASC (f) and UA (h) concentration (ranges from 0-0.1 mM) in PBS solutions with different pH values (pH∼5.7, 6.2, 7.4, 8.0).

Absorbance intensities at 240 nm (a) and fluorescence intensities at 430 nm (b) of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART solutions with GSH, L-Cys, ASC, UA, TAC or interfering species added. The CTRL is the UV–vis absorption intensity or fluorescence intensity of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART solutions.

The UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs mixed with GSH (concentration ranges from 0-0.1 mM) in artificial saliva. The embedded figures are the linear relationship between UV–vis absorbance intensity change and GSH concentration (a) or fluorescence intensity change and GSH concentration (b) in artificial saliva. The UV–vis absorption spectra (c) and fluorescence spectra (d) of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs mixed with water soluble total antioxidants in artificial saliva. The embedded figures are the linear relationship between UV–vis absorbance intensity change and total antioxidants concentration (c) or fluorescence intensity change and total antioxidants concentration (d) in artificial saliva.
(a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. (b) The antioxidants detection mechanism based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs in saliva.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The total antioxidant level is an important biomarker to determine redox imbalance in human body which can cause many diseases and threaten human health. Though the detection of antioxidants in vivo is gaining wide attention, most of the developed methods have only one detection mode and can only detect single type of antioxidant, leading to inaccurate test results. Here, we developed CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs as a dual-modal probe for water soluble total antioxidants detection in saliva. The detection mechanism was based on two reactions, including a cascading reaction of antioxidants activating the production of free radicals for fluorescence components generated, and a coordination reaction of antioxidants capturing Cu2+ from the MOFs nodes for carboxyl group protonated. Based on this, the UV–vis absorbance intensity and fluorescence intensity of the probes would be simultaneously changed after the incubation with antioxidants. Thus, the probe can detect antioxidants through both UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers. The dual-modal detection method can achieve rapid detection within 10 min with only 200 μL of samples used. Water soluble total antioxidants in saliva with component proportion fluctuated within a certain range can be well detected. And the obtained results were accurate with high recovery. This dual-modal probe provided an accurate, rapid and noninvasive method to detect water soluble total antioxidants in human body.

ce:section.ce:para: Redox imbalance in human body can cause oxidative stress which is the state with free radicals (Reactive Oxygen Species, ROS and Reactive Nitrogen Species, RNS) ROS/RNS overexpressed, as well as antioxidants level reduced. Meanwhile, oxidative stress can result in inflammatory infiltration of neutrophils, increased protease secretion, and the production of a large number of oxidative intermediates [1,2]. What’s more, long-term oxidative stress will induce various disease, including cancers, diabetes, Alzheimer, etc., bringing serious threat to human health [3–5]. Thus, the detection of redox level in the human body becomes particularly important to monitor the physical health. As antioxidants can resist oxidative stress by scavenging ROS and RNS [6,7], the level of total antioxidants including various enzymatic and non-enzymatic endogenous antioxidant compounds has been selected as an important biomarker for oxidative stress detection. At present, various analytical techniques, including UV–vis spectrophotometry [8–10], fluorescence spectrophotometry [11–13], electrochemical method [14–16], chromatography [17,18], etc. have been developed for the detection of antioxidants. Among them, UV–vis analysis and fluorescence detection are two extensively used techniques owing to their weak sophisticated instruments-dependence and easy operation. And in order to further improve the detection sensitivity, different nanoprobes have been developed to assist the detection [19–23]. However, the traditional nanoprobes designed for antioxidants detection usually have only a single detection mode. Even worse, most of the methods can’t simultaneously identify Single Electron Transfer (SET) working species (e.g., ascorbic acid) and Hydrogen Atom Transfer (HAT) working species (e.g., glutathione, cysteine) [24,25]. Since different antioxidants have a synergistic effect in human body, the detection of single species of antioxidant can’t reflect true redox levels in the body. Additionally, by using single-mode probes to detect antioxidants, the sensitivity and accuracy are easily confused by many factors, such as oxygen, metal ions, bacteria, etc. To overcome the above mentioned problems, it is urgently needed to develop a multi-modal probe to detect total antioxidant level in human body.

At present, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been widely used in the field of sensor and detection [26–28]. MOFs are crystalline porous materials with periodic network structure formed by self-assembly of metal ions and organic ligands [29–32]. Terephthalic acid (TA) is a widely used organic ligand to construct MOFs for different applications, especially for sensor technology [33]. Because TA can react with
OH to generate fluorescent product (2-hydroxyterephthalic acid) and Cu2+ can catalyze H2O2 into
OH, we have developed a H2O2-responsive fluorescence probe by coordinating Cu2+ with TA (CuBDC MOFs) for lysosomes imaging [34]. Besides, our group has found that the UV–vis absorption intensity belonging to carboxyl group in TA can be weakened after coordinating with Cu2+. While, the weakened absorbance intensity can be recovered by organic ligands which have stronger coordination ability to capture Cu2+ from the MOF nodes [35]. From the above analysis, it can be seen that CuBDC MOFs have the potential to act as dual-modal chemosensor for UV–vis and fluorescence analysis. Given that amino (-NH2) and sulfydryl (-SH) groups have strong coordination ability with Cu2+ [36,37], the CuBDC MOFs have the potential to detect antioxidants such as glutathione, uric acid, etc. through UV–vis spectrophotometry. Furthermore, we try to find a bridge based on free radicals, which can connect antioxidants with CuBDC MOFs to realize fluorescence detection of antioxidants. The Fe(III)-Artemisinin coordinated nanoparticles (Fe(III)-ART) synthesized by our group are selected because Fe(III)-ART NPs can generate free radicals activated by reducing components including some antioxidants [38]. In detailed, Fe3+ in Fe(III)-ART NPs can be reduced to Fe2+ by antioxidants, and Fe2+ further catalyzed the cleavage of peroxide bridge in artemisinin (ART) to produce free radicals [39]. Therefore, we are inspired to prepare dual-modal probe for total antioxidants detection by combining Fe(III)-ART NPs with CuBDC MOFs.

Water-soluble antioxidants, including glutathione (GSH), l-cysteine (L-Cys), uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (ASC) are the major components of antioxidants in human body. The above antioxidants collectively reflect the total antioxidants level which are closely related with the health status of the organism [40]. Additionally, it has been reported that the amount of antioxidants in saliva can be used to reflect the level of antioxidants in blood [41,42]. And saliva detection is simpler, more non-invasive, and more easily to collect samples than blood detection [6,40,43]. Herein, we developed a dual-modal probe to detect water-soluble total antioxidants in saliva through both UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The dual-modal probe was synthesized by depositing Fe(III)-ART shells on the surface of CuBDC MOFs (CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART) with using polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as adhesion agent (Scheme 1 ). The Fe(III)-ART shells would release Fe3+ and ART once encountered the water-soluble antioxidants. And the antioxidants can further reduce the released Fe3+ to Fe2+ which subsequently catalyzed ART to generate free radicals. TA in CuBDC MOFs can react with the generated free radicals to produce fluorescence components. On the other hand, Cu2+ in the MOFs nodes would be easily fetched out by -NH2 or -SH groups in the antioxidants, leading to the recovery of UV–vis absorbance intensity assigned to protonated carboxyl group. Therefore, the reaction between the probes and antioxidants can make UV–vis absorption and fluorescence intensity change simultaneously. Moreover, the unique core-shell structure can avoid CuBDC cores to contact with interferents directly, making the detection more specific. Benefiting from the above advantages, the detection of water soluble total antioxidants by CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs would be more accurate and sensitive.

Copper acetate (Cu(Ac)2·H2O), glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH), terephthalic acid (TA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), polyvinyl pyrrolidone K30 (PVP), glutathione (GSH), l-cysteine (L-Cys), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (ASC), urea, anhydrous ferric chloride (FeCl3) and ethanol (C2H5OH) were purchased from the Shanghai Chemical Factory, China. Artemisinin (ART) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biotech Company (Shanghai City, China). Dopamine hydrochloride, glucose, fructose and α-lactose were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai Macklin Chemical Factory, China). NaCl, CaCl2, KCl, MgCl2, ZnCl2, NH4Cl were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Tyrosine was purchased from Beijing Bailingwei Technology Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Cu(Ac)2 solution (0.01 mmol) was added to 40 mL of distilled water (containing PVP 1.2 g) and stirred for 30 min. Then NaOH solution containing TA (0.1 M) was added to the mixture dropwise and continued stirring for 1 h. The reaction was kept under static conditions at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the product was collected through centrifugation for 1 time and re-dispersed in distilled water.

Ethanol solution (50 mL) containing ART (0.1 M) was mixed with 4 mL of NaOH aqueous solution (0.02 g/mL) at 50 °C. After stirring for 30 min, the solution was diluted with distilled water and the pH value was adjusted to pH∼7. After that, 1 mL of FeCl3 (0.01 M) was added to the above CuBDC aqueous solution dropwise at room temperature and kept stirring for 30 min. Next, 1 mL of above ART (0.01 M) solution was also added to the mixture and kept stirring for another 1 h to form CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. And then, the solution was kept standing for about 3 h. Finally, the products were washed with distilled water, collected by centrifugation (6000 rpm for 5 min), and stored in distilled water at 4 °C.

CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs (200 μg/mL) were respectively mixed with GSH solution, l-Cys solution, ASC solution or UA solution (0.1 mM) for 10 min. Then the supernatant were collected by centrifugation. K3[Fe(CN)6] aqueous solution (0.2 %, 1 mL) were added into the above supernatant to identify the released iron ions and their valance state through UV–vis spectrophotometer.

CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs were mixed with GSH solution, l-Cys solution, ASC solution or UA solution, respectively. Then, 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were added into the above solution as free radical scavengers. Finally, the generation of free radicals was detected by electron spin resonance (ESR).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of antioxidants by CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs
ce:section.ce:para: For the evaluation of the selectivity of this proposed detection system based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs, ethanol, dopamine hydrochloride, tyrosine, α-lactose, glucose, fructose, urea, H2O2 and the following ions including Ca2+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Cl− and NH4 + were selected as interferents for this detection test. The concentrations of saccharides, ethanol and metal ions were 10 mM, dopamine hydrochloride, tyrosine were 0.2 mM, UA was 0.06 mM, H2O2, GSH, l-Cys and ASC were 0.1 mM, and total antioxidants were 0.0663 mM. The UV–vis absorption and the fluorescence spectra of the sensing system were investigated according to the experiments mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-modal detection of antioxidants based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, standard solution containing single antioxidant (GSH, l-Cys, ASC or UA) in the range of 0 mM-0.1 mM were freshly prepared. Then, CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs (50 μg/mL) was added to the standard solution and kept reacting for 10 min. After that, the mixture was analyzed by UV–vis absorption spectra (absorption peak at 240 nm) and fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength at 315 nm). To evaluate the feasibility of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs for analysis of real saliva, standard samples were prepared by dissolving antioxidants in artificial saliva with different concentrations. And the detection of antioxidants in saliva was performed according to the above method. The limit of detection was calculated as LOD = 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signals, k is the slope of a linear equation between UV–vis absorbance intensity and concentration of antioxidants or between fluorescence intensity and concentration of antioxidants).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the water soluble total antioxidant level by CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs in artifical saliva sample
ce:section.ce:para: Four kinds of antioxidants GSH, l-Cys, ASC and UA were selected to compose the water soluble total antioxidants. The amount of total antioxidants was calculated by adding different amount of single antioxidant together. It is reported that in the total antioxidants composed of saliva, uric acid accounts for more than 70 %, l-Cys and GSH accounts for about 15 % and 8 %, respectively, and ASC accounts for abount 0.8 % [44]. For this test assay, with the total amount of antioxidants fixed, the proportion of each antioxidant can fluctuate within the range that meets the above mentioned conditions of saliva. According to this principle, the standard samples were prepared by dissolving different amount of total antioxidants into artificial saliva (the concentration of total antioxidants is in the range of 0 mM-0.0667 mM). Then, the detection of total antioxidants was performed according to the above mentioned method.

Unstimulated saliva samples were collected from healthy male and female volunteers with the ages between 20 and 50 and at least 30 min after a meal or oral care procedure. Smokers were excluded from the study. The sample can be used without further processing [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water soluble total antioxidats in real saliva samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The prepared CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs (50 μg/mL) were dispersed in artificial saliva to act as detection reagent. 200 μL pure saliva or saliva containing extra antioxidant was added into the above detection agent. UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were measured according to the above mentioned method. Then Recovery and RSD were calculated based on the standard lines obtained from the detection of standard total antioxidants in artificial saliva. The Recovery and RSD are calculated according to following Eq.
Recovery= [C2 / (C1+C0)]*100 %

C2 (mM) stands for the concentration of total antioxidants in saliva. C1 (mM) stands for the concentration of artificially added total antioxidants. And C0 (mM) represents the concentration of total antioxidants in pure saliva.
RSD= [SD / X] *100 %.
The SD is the standard deviation, and the X represents the mean average of the calculated concentration of total oxidants [46].

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) characterizations were determined by using an X-ray diffractometer (D8-Advance) equipped with Cu-Kα radiation. Scanning electronic microscope (SEM, FEI Quanta 250 FEG) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 20) were used to characterize the morphologies of the materials. The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of samples were recorded on a Hitachi U4100 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a Hitachi F-4600 FL Spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were scanned by FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5). Zeta potential was collected from a Zetasizer Nano (Malvern Instruments). The hydrodynamic particle size was characterized with Malvern zetasizer Nanoseries (Nano ZS90). The concentration of iron ions was measured using inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (iCAP 7600 ICP-OES, ThermoFisher). The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were measured on Bruker EMX1598 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was obtained from ESCAlab250, Thermal Fischer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs, CuBDC cores were firstly synthesized through the coordination between Cu2+ and TA [38]. By mixing Cu2+ with a basic solution of TA under the template regulation of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), flower-like CuBDC MOFs with PVP modified were obtained. Then, Fe3+ was anchored on the surface of CuBDC NPs by coordinating with the amide moieties of PVP [47]. Finally, the Fe(III)-ART shells were formed on the surface of CuBDC by introducing hydrolysed ART to the CuBDC@PVP-Fe3+ solution ( Scheme 1 a).

SEM images showed that the lamellar building units were aggregated to form flower-like CuBDC MOFs with smooth surface (Fig. 1
a and b). After coated by Fe(III)-ART, the morphology of the MOFs didn’t have a significant change, but the surface became rather rough (Fig. 1 c and d). TEM image showed that CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs had an obvious core-shell structure with flower-like core and stacked shell, further confirmed the successful coating of Fe(III)-ART on the surface of CuBDC (Fig. 1 e-h). The EDS spectra showed that the signals of Cu, O, C and Fe elements were coexisted in the CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs (Fig. 1 i). Moreover, the corresponding EDS-elemental mapping demonstrated that all the elements were homogenously dispersed in the particles (Fig. 1 j), indicating the successful combination of CuBDC with Fe(III)-ART. Additionally, the geometric mean diameter of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs analyzed from the SEM images was about 0.9 ± 0.05 nm with relatively narrow size distribution (Fig. S1), as confirmed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. S2b).

The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern of the synthesized CuBDC MOFs (Fig. 1 k) matched well with the reported standard CuBDC [33]. And the diffraction peaks showed no significant change after coated by Fe(III)-ART (Fig. 1 k), owing to the amorphous form of Fe(III)-ART [38]. The Zeta potential of CuBDC MOFs was changed from −10.27 ± 0.23 eV to 1.04 ± 0.1 eV after the coating of Fe(III)-ART shells (Fig. S3). Compared to CuBDC MOFs, fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs had new peaks in the range of 2700∼3000 cm−1 assigned to C–H stretching vibration of ART (Fig. 1 l) [48]. Furthermore, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis for CuBDC MOFs and CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs showed that only the CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs had peaks at 711.4 eV assigned to the binding energy of Fe2p1/2 and Fe2p3/2 of Fe3+ (Fig. 1 m and n) [49]. All the above results further confirmed the successful coating of Fe(III)-ART on the surface of CuBDC MOFs. In addition, CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs prepared by this method are reddish-brown in water with good dispersion, and can be stored in water for more than 30 days at 4 °C (Fig. S4). This property makes it possible to detect antioxidants in water-based body fluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of antioxidants detection based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The antioxidants detection mechanism based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs was illustrated in Fig. 2
a. The antioxidants can trigger the release of Fe3+ from CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs, and further reduce Fe3+ into Fe2+. The reduced Fe2+ can catalyze the cleavage of peroxide-bridge in ART to generate free radicals [38]. Meanwhile, TA in CuBDC can interact with the generated free radicals to produce fluorescence product. Additionally, Cu2+ ions, the reactive metal center in the framework, can be fetched from the CuBDC MOF nodes by active groups in antioxidants, inducing protonation of carboxyl groups, accompanied by the recovery of UV–vis absorbance at 240 nm. To better reveal the detection mechanism, the change of iron valence state after mixing with antioxidants was firstly determined by K3[Fe(CN)6] [50]. In detailed, the solution containing CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs and GSH was centrifuged and the supernatant was extracted to mix with K3[Fe(CN)6]. Then, the color of K3[Fe(CN)6] changed from yellow to green (Fig. 2 b), indicating the existence of Fe2+. In addition, the mixture of K3[Fe(CN)6] and the supernatant was also investigated by UV–vis. As shown in Fig. 2 b, a new absorption peak around 700 nm appeared. This peak was assigned to prussian blue which was generated from the reaction between Fe2+ and K3[Fe(CN)6], further proved the existence of Fe2+. Meanwhile, the ability of other antioxidants (L-Cys, ASC or UA) to cause Fe3+ released and reduced was also evaluated by the above methods. The results (Fig. 2 b) demonstrated that all the selected antioxidants can make K3[Fe(CN)6] changed from yellow to green with new absorption peak generated. The degree of color change and the generated absorption intensity are closely related to the reducing capacity of the antioxidants (Fig. 2 b). Considering that Fe(III)-ART shells only containing trivalent irons [38], the detection of Fe2+ in the supernatant indicated that antioxidants can trigger the release of Fe3+ from CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs, and subsequently reduced Fe3+ into Fe2+. In addition, FTIR spectra showed that the peak at ∼2552 cm−1 assigned to -SH group in GSH (Fig. 2 c) or L-Cys (Fig. S5a), the peak in the range of 3300∼3500 cm−1 assigned to -NH group in UA (Fig. S5b) and the peak in the range of 3200∼3650 cm−1 assigned to OH group in ASC (Fig. S5c) have significant changes after interacting with CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART. These results further suggested the occurrence of redox reaction between CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART and antioxidants. According to our previous work [38], the resulting Fe2+ can react with ART to produce carbon-centered free radicals. Additionally, it has been reported that the ring hydrogen in TA can be abstracted and replaced by a hydroxyl radical to produce fluorescent substances, 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid [33]. Based on this, we hypothesized that the generated carbon-centered free radicals can also replace the ring hydrogen in TA, leading to the production of fluorescence compounds. In order to prove our conjecture, fluorescence analysis were performed and the results (Fig. S6) showed that TA solution or mixture solution containing TA and ART are non-fluorescent. While the mixture solution containing TA, FeSO4 and ART generated fluorescence peak around 430 nm. Furthermore, the mixture of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART and GSH was measured by electron spin resonance (ESR) with using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as radical scavenger. The obtained ESR signal was quite different from the signal assigned to carbon-centered free radicals which was generated by the system containing Fe(III)-ART and GSH (Fig. 2 d) [38]. In addition, the ESR signals generated from the mixture composed of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART and other antioxidants (L-Cys, ASC or UA) were also different from the signals assigned to carbon-centered free radicals (Fig. S7). All the above results indicated that the antioxidants can cause Fe(III)-ART to generate free radicals which further react with TA in CuBDC to produce fluorescence products.

To prove that antioxidants can capture Cu2+ from the CuBDC nodes and inducing recovery of the UV–vis absorption intensity assigned to protonated carboxyl group, the UV–vis spectra of the TA solution, CuBDC solution and CuBDC solution mixed with GSH were evaluated. The results showed that (Fig. S8) TA had an obvious absorption peak at 240 nm caused by the carboxyl groups, while the absorption intensity was greatly weakened in the CuBDC solution due to the coordination between Cu2+ and carboxyl group in TA. Interestingly, with the addition of GSH, the absorption intensity of CuBDC solution was recovered. Additionally, PXRD characterization was performed to further confirm that Cu2+ in CuBDC nodes can be fetched out by antioxidants. Compared with CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs, the products from the reaction between CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART and antioxidants (GSH, L-Cys or UA) had new diffraction peaks (Fig. 2 e). This result indicated that these three antioxidants can capture Cu2+ from CuBDC MOFs, inducing the formation of new substances. However, the diffraction peak of product from the reaction between CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART and ASC was almost the same as that of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART. This result suggested that ASC can’t capture Cu2+ from CuBDC due to its lack of a functional group to coordinate with copper ions strongly. Though such characteristic may preclude the UV–vis detection of ASC based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs, the degradation of Fe(III)-ART caused by ASC can help to overcome this problem. The detailed results and discussions would be shown in later part of the article. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed to further determine the chemical construction changes of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs induced by antioxidants. High-resolution Cu2p spectra (Fig. S9a, b and d) showed that after interacting with GSH, L-Cys or UA, the half-peak full width (FWHM) of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs was slightly expanded accompanied by the peak shift. However, the XPS spectra of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs didn’t have obvious change after interacting with ASC (Fig. S9c), which further indicated that ASC can’t catch Cu2+ from CuBDC nodes. In further study, the Cu2p spectra of the product from the reaction between CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs and GSH (Fig. 2 f) or L-Cys (Fig. 2 g) were resolved into peaks belonging to Cu-O (Be = 933.4 eV and 953.6 eV) and Cu-S (Be = 932.6 eV and 952.8 eV), respectively [51–53], indicating the fracture of Cu-O with the formation of Cu-S instead. Meanwhile, the Cu2p spectrum of the product from the reaction between CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs and UA (Fig. 2 h) was resolved into peaks assigned to Cu-O (Be = 933.4 eV and 953.6 eV) and Cu-N (Be = 934.6 eV and 954.6 eV), respectively [54], indicating the breakage of Cu-O with the generation of Cu-N instead. All the above results suggested that the CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs can be used to detect antioxidants through both fluorescence spectrophotometry and UV–vis spectrophotometry, based on the mechanism of fluorescent substance generation and carboxyl groups protonation. For this property, the accuracy of antioxidants detection can be significantly improved compared to traditional techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and UV–vis spectral detection of antioxidants in aqueous solution based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
To get the best detection performance of the probes, the effect of Fe(III)-ART shell thickness on the detection was firstly investigated. CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs was synthesized with different Fe(III)-ART shell thickness by adjusting the mole ratio of FeCl3 to artemisinin (FeCl3 : ART = 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:4). Then, the synthesized NPs were used to detect standard GSH solution through UV–vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer, simultaneously. Fig. S10 showed that the probes synthesized by fixing the mole ratio of FeCl3 to artemisinin at 1:1 had the highest detection sensitivity and wide detection range. Therefore, these NPs were selected as optimal probe to detect water soluble antioxidants. Furthermore, the mole ratio of Fe3+ to ART in these CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs were about 1:2.1 as determined by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-OES). On the other hand, the optimal reaction time for this test assay was also evaluated. In detailed, the solution of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs incubated with different antioxidants was monitored by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra at predetermined times. As shown in Fig. S11-S14, the UV–vis absorption signal of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs were rapidly enhanced within 10 min after incubation with GSH (Fig. S11a and b), L-Cys(Fig. S12a and b) or UA (Fig. S14a and b), and kept slowly increased after that. As for ASC (Fig. S13a and b), the UV–vis absorption signal of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs showed a significant increase within 5 min after incubation with the antioxidants, and subsequently, the signal had a negligible change. From the fluorescence tests, it can be seen that the antioxidants of GSH (Fig. S11c and d) and L-Cys (Fig. S12c and d) caused the fluorescence signal of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs fast increase in the first 10 min of incubation, and then the increase rate was reduced. As for ASC (Fig. S13c and d) and UA (Fig. S14c and d), the fluorescence signal of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs had a fast increase in the first 5 min of incubation, and subsequently, the increase rate slowed down. These results showed that both Single Electron Transfer (SET) working species and Hydrogen Atom Transfer (HAT) working species can cause UV–vis and fluorescence signal of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs to have significant change within 10 min of incubation. This is an important feature to make it possible for CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs to detect total antioxidants, including HAT working species and SET working species. Combined with the working efficiency required in practical application, the reaction time between the probes and samples was set at 10 min in this test assay. Under optimized conditions, four kinds of water-soluble antioxidants in aqueous solution were detected by this proposed assay. Take GSH as an example, the UV–vis absorption peak at 240 nm were gradually increased with the increase of GSH concentration (Fig. 3
a). An excellent linear relationship between △A 240 and GSH concentration was established in the range of 0∼0.1 mM (△A 240 = 14.66 [GSH] - 0.186, R 2 = 0.98) (Fig. 3 b). Meanwhile, the increasing amounts of GSH added to CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs also induced a continuous fluorescence intensity (at 430 nm) increase (Fig. 3 c). And a good linear relationship was established between △F 430 and GSH concentration ranging from 0 to 0.1 mM (△F 430 = 21135.655 [GSH] + 490.752, R² = 0.99) (Fig. 3 d). All the above results indicated that this test assay had the potential for a quantitative signal readout using both UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. In addition, the linearity, reproducibility and limit of detection for other antioxidants detection were also determined by this method and the test results were shown in Fig. S15-S17. As can be seen, both UV–vis and fluorescence spectra showed the good detection performance for l-Cys (Fig. S15) and ASC (Fig. S16) with strong linearity in the concentration range of 0∼0.1 mM. And the detection for UA (Fig. S17) had strong linearity in the concentration range of 0∼0.06 mM. These results demonstrated that by using CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs as probes, both SET working species and HAT working species can be well detected in the concentration ranging from 0 to 0.06 mM in water solution. It is worth noting that the UV–vis absorption intensity for ASC (Fig. S16a) detection had less changes compared to that for other antioxidants detection, due to the weak ability of ASC to capture Cu2+ from the MOF nodes. Given that the decomposition of Fe(III)-ART shells can make more CuBDC exposed, thus, the absorption change for ASC detection was caused by the exposed CuBDC rather than carboxyl group protonation. However, the fluorescence intensity showed a significant enhancement with ASC concentration increased (Fig. S16c). And combining the sensor signal of UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra, ASC can be well determined. Thus, it can be seen that multimodal detection can make up for the deficiency of single mode detection, inducing the improvement of detection accuracy.

Considering the practical application in human body, the influence of pH values on the antioxidants detection based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs was studied. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) with different pH values (pH = 5.7, pH = 6.2, pH = 7.4, pH = 8.0) were employed. Then, GSH, l-Cys, ASC and UA with various concentrations were detected by CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs in the above PBS. The UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra (GSH (Fig. S18-S21), l-Cys (Fig. S22-S25), ASC (Fig. S26-S29) and UA (Fig. S30-S33)) showed that for all the above mentioned antioxidants, good relationships between UV–vis intensity change △A 240 and antioxidants concentration ranging from 0 to 0.1 mM were established under different pH values (GSH (Fig. 4
a), L-Cys (Fig. 4 c), ASC (Fig. 4 e) and UA (Fig. 4 g)). The same is true for the relationship between fluorescence intensity change △F 430 and antioxidants concentration ranging from 0 to 0.1 mM (GSH (Fig. 4 b), L-Cys (Fig. 4 d),ASC (Fig. 4 f) and UA (Fig. 4 h)). These results revealed that the CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs can be adapted for antioxidants detection in a wide pH range of 5.0∼8.0, which is suitable for detection in human body fluids.

Furthermore, the selectivity of this proposed probe was evaluated in the presence of some potentially co-existed interfering substances, including Ca2+, K+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Cl−, NH4 +, alcohols (E.g. ethanol), amines (E.g. urea, dopamine), amino acids (E.g. tyrosine), some saccharides (α-lactose, glucose, fructose) and free radicals (E.g. H2O2). Under optimum experimental conditions, Fig. 5
a and b showed that both the absorption intensity and fluorescence intensity of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs had negligible changes upon the addition of interfering substances other than the antioxidants, even when the concentration of interferents is higher than that of antioxidants (GSH, L-Cys, ASC and UA). The results indicated that though some molecules in human body including ethanol, urea, dopamine, and saccharides (α-lactose, glucose and fructose) had a suitable redox potential to reduce Fe3+, they had less influence on the UV–vis and fluorescence signal change of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. The reasons can be attributed to the following two aspects. First, the redox potential of the above molecules can’t support them to reduce enough Fe3+ into Fe2+ in a short time. Owing to this result, the free radicals generated from the interaction between Fe2+ and ART were insufficient to enhance the fluorescence intensity of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs, significantly. Second, the above molecules have weak coordination ability with Cu2+. Thus, they could not capture Cu2+ from CuBDC MOFs to recover the UV–vis absorption intensity assigned to uncoordinated carboxyl groups. It has been reported that H2O2 can enhance the fluorescence signal of CuBDC because Cu2+ in CuBDC can catalyze H2O2 into
OH which then interact with TA to generate fluorescence compounds [34]. However, Fig. 5 showed that H2O2 can enhance neither UV–vis signal nor fluorescence signal of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. This phenomenon can be attributed to the core-shell structure of the CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. In detailed, the unchanged UV–vis signal of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs incubated with H2O2 (Fig. 5 a) demonstrated that H2O2 can’t make Fe(III)-ART shells decomposed, let alone CuBDC cores exposed. Additionally, the release of Fe3+ from CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs in the presence of H2O2 was tested by K₄[Fe(CN)₆] which can interact with Fe3+ to generate turnbull blue. The results (Fig. S34) showed that the color of K₄[Fe(CN)₆] didn’t have a significant change after mixing with the supernate extract from solution containing CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs and H2O2, further confirmed un-decomposition of Fe(III)-ART shells. Thus, the Fe(III)-ART shells can protect the CuBDC cores from contacting with H2O2 directly. The interaction between CuBDC and H2O2 was then limited, leading to less fluorescence compounds generated. To confirm this hypothesis, the CuBDC NPs and Fe(III)-ART NPs were mixed in proportion according to the components ratio of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs. And then, UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of the mixture were investigated after incubating with H2O2 for 10 min. Fig. S35 showed that the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was obviously increased compared to that of solution containing CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs and H2O2. The above results indicated that the core-shell structure are necessary for CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs to realize specific and accurate detection. Thus, the CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs have potential to apply in the detection of antioxidants in saliva.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water soluble total antioxidants in Saliva
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to evaluate the feasibility of the dual-modal probe for antioxidants detection in saliva, the determination of single antioxidant was firstly performed in artificial saliva. The UV–vis absorbance spectra and fluorescence spectra (GSH (Fig. 6
a and b), L-Cys (Fig. S36a and c), UA (Fig. S37a and c) and ASC (Fig. S38a and c)) demonstrated that the complex biological matrix in artificial saliva had negligible effects on the detection results. All the antioxidants (GSH (Fig. 6 a and b), L-Cys (Fig. S36b and d), UA (Fig. S37b and d) and ASC (Fig. S38b and d)) showed concentration-dependent readings in the range of 0−0.1 mM in artificial saliva with low limit of detection (LOD). For UV–vis spectra detection, the LOD of GSH (Fig. 6a), L-Cys (Fig. S36b), UA (Fig. S37b) and ASC (Fig. S38b) are respectively 0.00205 mM, 0.00195 mM, 0.00182 mM and 0.00110 mM. Meanwhile, for fluorescence spectra detection, the LOD of GSH (Fig. 6b), L-Cys (Fig. S36d), UA (Fig. S37d) and ASC (Fig. S38d) are respectively 0.00313 mM, 0.00359 mM, 0.00209 mM and 0.00643 mM. The LOD of CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART nanoprobe for antioxidants detection is equivalent to or lower than those of previously reported sensors for antioxidants detection [55,56].

The dual-modal probe was then applied to analyze water soluble total antioxidants in artificial saliva. The concentration of total antioxidants was calculated by adding different amount of single antioxidant (GSH, L-Cys, ASC and UA) together. It has been reported that uric acid accounts for more than 70 % of the water soluble total antioxidants. The contributions of L-Cys and GSH are about 15 % and 8 %, respectively. And the contribution of ASC is the smallest, around 0.8 % [44]. According to this principle, the standard samples with different amount of total antioxidants dissolved in artificial saliva were analyzed by the probe through both UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 6 c and d and Table S1, the concentration of total antioxidants was positively correlated with the absorbance intensity or fluorescence intensity, and a good linear relationship was established (△A 240 = 5.382 [GSH] + 0.197, R² = 0.92, △F 430 = 6072.984 [GSH] + 144.43, R² = 0.95).

As for real saliva samples, the contribution ratio of individual antioxidant to total antioxidants usually fluctuates within a certain range, even if the amount of total antioxidants is constant. Thus, the influence of such proportion fluctuation on the quantitative detection of total antioxidants needed to be evaluated. Under the premise that UA usually contributes more than 70 % of the total water soluble antioxidants in saliva, total antioxidants with different proportions were prepared for analysis. In detailed, the total antioxidants had fixed concentration at 0.0295 mM, in which the proportion of UA was varied in the range of 65 %–75 %, and the proportion of the other three antioxidants was also changed within a certain range (Table S2). Investigated by the proposed probe, Table S3 showed that the UV–vis absorbance intensity and fluorescence intensity of the above samples had negligible changes. Meanwhile, we have also evaluated the possibility of this probe for the detection of total antioxidants with proportion of each component arbitrarily changed (Table S4). However, Table S5 showed that the total antioxidants with fixed concentration have different detection signal when the ratio of each component (GSH: UA: ASC: L-Cys) changed from 1:1:1:1 to 2:2:1:1 (Table S4). From the above results, it can draw a conclusion as follows. On the premise that UA contributes 65 %–75 % of the water soluble total antioxidants in saliva, the linear relationship between total antioxidants concentration and △A2
40 or △F 430 shown in Fig. 6 c and d was available for the detection of total antioxidants in real saliva.

Encouraged by the above results, the probes were used to measure water soluble total antioxidants in real saliva samples. Though it has been proved that the CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs can be used to detect antioxidants in a wide pH range of 5.0∼8.0, the standard curves obtained in different pH values are not the same. To avoid protracting different standard curves for different saliva samples, artificial saliva with weak pH buffering capacity was selected to act as solvent for this test assay. Standard solutions was obtained by dissolving different amount of total antioxidants in artificial saliva as mentioned above and standard curve was shown in Fig. 6 c and d. To further reduce the effect of real saliva on the pH value of the artificial saliva, the test sample was prepared by dispersing only 200 μL of real saliva samples into 2 mL of artificial saliva. After CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs added into the above test sample, the mixture was measured by UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. Based on the linear standard regression equation (Fig. 6 c and d), the concentration of water soluble total antioxidants in real saliva sample was calculated to be 0.0047 mM (UV–vis mode) and 0.0055 mM (fluorescence mode), respectively. It can be seen that the quantitative detection results by these two modes are very close, further indicated the feasibility of the dual-modal probes for antioxidants detection in saliva. Then, the reliability of this method was evaluated by recovery tests which quantitatively detected the real saliva samples with extra three different concentrations of total antioxidants added. The Recovery and Relative Standard Deviation ( RSD ) of the samples were summarized in Table 1 . The results showed that the recoveries of the samples measured by UV–vis spectra ranged from 103.3 %–116.0 %, and measured by fluorescence spectra ranged from 105.2 %–109.8 %, respectively. The relative standard deviation was in the range of 2.8 %–4.4 % (UV–vis mode) and 1.3 %–2.3 % (fluorescence mode), separately. Moreover, for the same sample, the amount of total antioxidants determined by UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra are very close. The analytical results (Table 1 ) indicated that the dual-modal probes based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs are accurate, feasible, fast responsive and few samples needed for the detection of water soluble total antioxidants in saliva.

In summary, a dual-modal probe based on CuBDC@Fe(III)-ART NPs is designed to detect water soluble total antioxidants in saliva. Owing to the core-shell structure, the detection mechanism was involved a cascade reaction between antioxidants and Fe(III)-ART shells, and a coordination reaction between antioxidants and CuBDC cores. Such two complementary factors enable the probe to detect antioxidants through both UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometer. By using this probe, both SET working species and HAT working species can be detected in artificial saliva with a wide linear range of 0−0.1 mM. Significantly, the possibility to use this test assay for real saliva samples detection was demonstrated by analyzing 200 μL of real saliva samples with high recovery obtained. Compared to traditional TAC detection method based on Fe3+ or Cu2+, this test assay can simultaneously detect SET working species and HAT working species. Additionally, it was easy to operate by using only one kind of detection reagent and a small amount of samples. More importantly, dual-modal detection can be realized by this method, leading to more accurate test results. In general, this dual-modal probe has potential to apply in disease diagnosis related to the role of total antioxidants biomarker.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xue Wei is a second-year graduate student in School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Henan University of Science and Technology, majoring in Chemical Engineering. Her main research interest is development of metal-organic frame material for chemical sensing.

Jian Chen received her B.S. in Materials Science and Engineering, South West University in 2010, followed by her pH.D. in Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology of China in 2015. Dr. Chen is currently an Associate Professor in Henan Key Laboratory of Nanocomposite and Applications, Institute of Nanostructured Functional Materials, Huanghe Science and Technology College. Her research interests include development of chemical sensors, molecular imaging probe and nanocatalyst for cancer therapy.
Xinyu Zhang is a junior undergraduate in Institute of Nanostructured Functional Materials, Huanghe Science and Technology College. Her research interests include development of nanomaterials.
Ziqing Zhu is a junior undergraduate in Institute of Nanostructured Functional Materials, Huanghe Science and Technology College. Her research interests include development of nanomaterials.

Huili Liu received her M.S in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, China West Normal University in 2013. She is currently a technician in Henan Key Laboratory of Nanocomposite and Applications, Institute of Nanostructured Functional Materials, Huanghe Science and Technology College. Her research interests include development of materials and characterization.

Xinsheng Wang received his B.S. in College of Life Sciences, Henan Normal University in 2002, followed by his pH.D. from Wuhan Botanical Garden, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2013. Dr. Wang is currently an Associate Professor in School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Henan University of Science and Technology. His research interests include identification of chemical constituents of traditional Chinese medicine.

Xuming Guo received his pH.D. in organocatalysis from Shandong University in 2008. Dr. Guo is currently the Director of Analysis and Testing Center of School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy of Henan University of Science and Technology. Dr. Guo’s research interests are preparation of new chemical materials and instrumental analysis of compound and mixture.

Baocheng Yang received his B.S. in College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University in 1991. He then received his M.S. in College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Peking University, followed by his pH.D. in Chemical Engineering, University of Connecticut in 2002. Dr. Yang is currently a Professor in Henan Key Laboratory of Nanocomposite and Applications, Institute of Nanostructured Functional Materials, Huanghe Science and Technology College. His research interests include functional nanomaterials and sensor technology.
